name: sec-audit
description: v1.23 makes sec-audit usable at PR time: `--sarif` emits a GitHub-code-scanning-compatible SARIF 2.1.0 log beside the markdown report (deterministic `scripts/secaudit/sarif.py`, the pipeline's first machine-readable deliverable; severity→level, CVSS/score→security-severity, sentinels filtered, secrets never surfaced), and `--diff[=ref]` scopes the whole review to changed files (`scripts/secaudit/diffscope.py` computes the changed set; `inventory.py`/`runner.py` gain `--files` scoping; runner now prunes `.git`/vendored dirs). v1.22 adds EPSS (FIRST.org exploit-probability) enrichment: `cve-enricher` batch-fetches EPSS scores (100 CVE ids/GET, only ids leave the machine) after the KEV pass and attaches `epss` / `epss_percentile` to every CVE; the §5 exploit sub-score becomes graded (KEV=20 / EPSS≥0.5=15 / EPSS≥0.1=10 / PoC=10 / else 0, max unchanged so buckets don't shift), and the report renders EPSS on each CVE line plus a Max-EPSS column. v1.21 adds the `secrets` lane — gitleaks (working-tree scan with `--redact`) + trufflehog (git-history scan with `--no-verification`, so no credential is ever tested against a live service) — detecting leaked credentials (CWE-798) across every project; gitleaks always scans the working tree while trufflehog scans git history when the target is a repo (else a `no-git-history` clean-skip). Evidence is always the redacted match, never the raw secret (enforced by a canary in the e2e). v1.21 also lands the project's own CI: a GitHub Actions workflow running the hermetic test suite (`tests/ci-local.sh`) on push/PR. v1.20 converts the `virt` lane to the deterministic runner engine via a new per-file pass/fail `validator` mode (exit-code → synthesized finding, with `line_regex` + `dedupe_by`) plus list-valued `applicable_glob` and multi-glob `{files:a|b}` support — hadolint maps with existing engine features and virt-xml-validate runs through the validator mode, so 16 of the runner lanes are now deterministic script-backed. The COVERAGE "still agent-backed" set is reclassified by benefit: planned-conversion (ai-tools — validator mode now exists, still needs a field `coalesce` op + a golden fixture), parked (linux — pending a table-parsing mode), and permanently agent-backed by design (macos/ios/windows host-OS-gated signing tools + netcfg config validators, where conversion saves no per-finding LLM cost). v1.19 adds the engine `cvss_band` transform and `input_format: xml` adapter, converting the rust (cargo-audit/deny/geiger) and android (mobsfscan/apkleaks/android-lint XML) lanes — 15 of the 22 runner lanes are now deterministic script-backed; the remaining 7 (ai-tools jq-validation, netcfg/virt validators, linux/macos/ios/windows command-output + host-OS-gated) stay LLM agents by design. v1.18 extends the v1.17 deterministic-runner engine with filter specs, nested/multi-source flatten, dict-iteration, and substring transforms, converting four more lanes (supply-chain, k8s, webext, webapp) to scripts — 13 of the runner lanes are now script-backed (only android/rust/ai-tools and the validator/desktop lanes remain LLM agents, by design). v1.17 moves the deterministic parts of the pipeline into stdlib-Python scripts under scripts/secaudit/ (no third-party deps): cve_enricher.py (OSV/NVD/GHSA/KEV + MAL- classification), score.py (the §5 prioritization rubric), inventory.py (the §2 file-glob/content-grep detection pre-pass), and a config-driven runner engine (runner.py + lanes/<lane>.json) that deterministically maps tool-native JSON to the finding schema for the sast, go, shell, ansible, gh-actions, python, iac, image, and dast lanes. Those agents become thin wrappers that run the script and apply a presentation-only polish (hybrid model), eliminating per-finding fabrication risk while preserving the JSONL/sentinel/origin contract (contract-check + drills + e2e unchanged). Lanes with multi-array or non-JSON tools (k8s, webext, webapp, supply-chain, android, rust, ai-tools, netcfg, virt, linux, macos, ios, windows) remain LLM runner agents. Cybersecurity review plugin covering web services, servers, browser extensions, Rust projects, Go projects, Python projects, Ansible playbooks, shell scripts (bash/sh/dash/ksh), networking-as-code (Tor torrc, WireGuard, sing-box, Xray), container image vulnerability scanning (trivy + grype on image tarballs / OCI layouts / SBOMs — the OSS-equivalent of Docker Scout's CVE surface without Docker-daemon / registry-pull dependencies), AI coding tool configuration (Claude Code plugins / agents / skills / commands / hooks / MCP servers, Cursor rules, Codex agents, OpenCode agents — covering prompt injection, tool poisoning (Invariant Labs / OWASP class), rug-pull supply-chain via unpinned npx/uvx, MCP server name shadowing / homoglyph in mcpServers keys, sampling-capability declaration, OAuth 2.0 + PKCE on HTTPS MCP per RFC 9700, confused-deputy blast-radius across multi-MCP setups, allowed-tools wildcards, hardcoded credentials, dangerous hooks, alwaysApply rule scoping, codex sandbox/approval policy — now also running mcp-scan inspect (Invariant Labs / Snyk Agent Scan) for tool-poisoning detection alongside jq for structural validation), Android apps, iOS apps, desktop Linux, desktop macOS, desktop Windows, Kubernetes manifests, Terraform/Pulumi IaC, GitHub Actions workflows, and virtualization / alternative-container-runtime configuration (Docker daemon, Podman / Quadlet, libvirt / QEMU / KVM, Apple Containers, UTM) via a pipelined twenty-seven-agent architecture (sec-expert, finding-triager, cve-enricher, sast-runner, dast-runner, webext-runner, rust-runner, android-runner, ios-runner, linux-runner, macos-runner, windows-runner, k8s-runner, iac-runner, gh-actions-runner, virt-runner, go-runner, shell-runner, python-runner, ansible-runner, netcfg-runner, image-runner, ai-tools-runner, webapp-runner, supply-chain-runner, dep-diff-analyst, report-writer). v1.16 adds the opt-in `--deep-deps` release-diff pass — the sonnet-pinned dep-diff-analyst agent fetches a flagged dependency's installed version N and prior version N-1 registry-natively (PyPI JSON API + npm registry, no pip/npm install), runs a bounded `diff -ruN`, and classifies the release benign / suspicious / malicious with diff-quoted evidence (origin `deep-deps`, tool `dep-diff`, a `verdict` field). Candidates come from the supply-chain lane + cve-enricher's MAL- array, capped at `deep_deps_max` (default 10); this is the point-in-time port of elastic/supply-chain-monitor's release-diff classification and the only signal that catches a compromised release of an otherwise-legitimate package. v1.15 adds the `supply-chain` lane — guarddog (Datadog, Apache-2.0; heuristic malicious-package scanner for PyPI + npm: install-time code execution, obfuscation, download-and-exec, exfiltration, typosquatting / dependency confusion) + osv-scanner (Google, Apache-2.0; full lockfile-graph resolution reporting OSV `MAL-` malicious-package advisories incl. transitives, filtered to MAL- only so CVEs are not double-reported) — detecting malicious dependencies (CWE-506 embedded malicious code, CWE-94 code injection, CWE-494 download-without-integrity, CWE-200 exfiltration, CWE-1357 typosquat/dependency-confusion, CWE-1007 trojan-source); paired with cve-enricher now classifying OSV `MAL-` + GHSA `type: malware` advisories (returned by its existing OSV/GHSA calls, no extra requests) into a per-package `malicious` array promoted to CRITICAL. Inspired by elastic/supply-chain-monitor's release-diff malware-classification approach, adapted point-in-time to the audited project's own dependency set. v1.14 adds the `webapp` lane — bearer (Apache-2.0; cross-language SAST tuned for OWASP Top 10 + data-flow tracking; supports JS/TS/Java/Ruby/PHP/Go/Python) + njsscan (MIT; Node.js-specific MobSF-family scanner) + brakeman (MIT; Rails-only deep SAST) — covering SQL injection (CWE-89), SSRF (CWE-918), XXE (CWE-611), path traversal (CWE-22), file upload (CWE-434), open redirect (CWE-601), SSTI (CWE-1336), mass assignment (CWE-915), IDOR / broken access control (CWE-639/CWE-285), prototype pollution (CWE-1321), command injection in web context (CWE-78), HTTP request smuggling / CORS misconfig / host-header injection (CWE-444/CWE-942/CWE-644), and insecure deserialization (CWE-502). v1.10 UX improvements: invocation defaults to current working directory when no path is supplied; inventory pass detects known-but-uncovered technologies and surfaces them as Coverage-gap suggestions in the report with model pinning — sonnet-grade code reasoning, haiku-grade CVE feed I/O and eight-lane tool dispatch. Multi-stack dispatch is the default: when a project satisfies ≥2 inventory keys (Tauri apps = Rust + webext + desktop OS; Flutter apps = Android + iOS; React-Native apps = Android + iOS + webext), all applicable runners dispatch in parallel and findings render in separate per-lane sections. The `/sec-audit` slash command accepts `--only=<lanes>` / `--skip=<lanes>` flags for selective dispatch; the final report includes a per-lane summary table and Lane-filter-applied metadata. Citation-grounded expertise across databases, frameworks, webservers (incl. Windows/IIS), proxies, frontend, browser extensions (Chrome MV3 + Firefox AMO), Rust/Cargo ecosystem + unsafe surface, Android (manifest + data + runtime), iOS (Info.plist + data + codesign), desktop Linux (systemd + sandboxing + packaging), desktop macOS (hardened runtime + TCC + .pkg/Sparkle), desktop Windows (PE hardening + Authenticode + AppLocker/WDAC + MSI/MSIX), auth, TLS, containers, secrets, and supply-chain — all primary-source-cited. Live CVE enrichment from NVD 2.0, OSV.dev (native for crates.io/Maven/NuGet/PyPI/npm/Go/RubyGems/Packagist; best-effort via GHSA for CocoaPods/SwiftPM/Debian), and GHSA with CISA KEV cross-reference. Structured skipped-list primitive distinguishes ten canonical clean-skip reasons (7 target-shape: no-apk/no-bundle/no-pkg/no-debian-source/no-elf/no-systemd-unit/no-pe; 3 host-OS-gated: requires-macos-host/requires-systemd-host/requires-windows-host; 1 profile-absent: no-notary-profile) from tool failures. `references/COVERAGE.md` is the single source of truth enumerating every lane, ecosystem, and skip reason. Per-run token-cost accounting; offline-degradation drills for CVE feeds and all eight tool lanes; prioritized markdown report with verbatim-quoted primary-source fixes.
